statement: Iceland's engagement with the EU ETS has followed the general EEA EFTA timeline rather than involving early or proactive negotiation. Key milestones: The EU ETS Directive (2003/87/EC) was adopted by the EU in October 2003. EEA Joint Committee Decision No 146/2007 incorporating the Directive into the EEA Agreement was adopted on 26 October 2007, entering into force on 29 December 2007 — over four years after the Directive's adoption. Iceland began participating in the EU ETS from 1 January 2008 (Phase II). The aviation amendment (Directive 2008/101/EC) was incorporated into the EEA Agreement through subsequent Joint Committee Decisions. Iceland's negotiations regarding exemptions and special treatment for its aviation sector came primarily in connection with the Fit for 55 package (proposed July 2021, adopted 2023), when Iceland sought and obtained the free allocation extension through 2026. The EEA EFTA states (Iceland, Norway, Liechtenstein) participate in the EEA Joint Committee process for incorporating EU legislation but do not have voting rights in the EU legislative process where the ETS rules are decided. They can raise concerns through the EEA consultation mechanisms but cannot formally amend EU legislation. Whether earlier engagement would have produced better outcomes is a counterfactual question that cannot be definitively answered.
source_name: EFTA — Climate policy and the EEA Agreement; EEA Joint Committee records; European Commission
caveats: The claim that 'earlier Icelandic governments should have engaged earlier in ETS negotiations' is a normative/political judgment rather than a factual claim. The EEA EFTA states have limited formal influence over EU legislation — they can submit comments during the EU legislative process and negotiate adaptations when incorporating legislation into the EEA Agreement, but they cannot vote or formally amend directives. The four-year gap between the EU ETS Directive (2003) and its EEA incorporation (2007) partly reflects the complexity of the EEA decision-shaping process and constitutional requirements in the EFTA states. Whether this constituted a failure of engagement or simply the normal pace of EEA incorporation is a matter of political interpretation.

related_entries.statement: Iceland participates in the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) through the EEA Agreement, not through a separate bilateral agreement. The EU ETS Directive (2003/87/EC) was incorporated into the EEA Agreement through EEA Joint Committee Decision No 146/2007 of 26 October 2007, which inserted the Directive as point 21al of Annex XX (Environment) to the EEA Agreement. Constitutional requirements were indicated by Iceland, Liechtenstein, and Norway; these were fulfilled by 28 December 2007, and the decision entered into force on 29 December 2007. Iceland, Norway, and Liechtenstein have participated in the EU ETS since 1 January 2008 (the start of Phase II) on the same legal basis as EU Member States. Subsequent amendments to the ETS Directive — including the aviation inclusion (Directive 2008/101/EC), the Phase III reforms, and the Fit for 55 revisions — have also been incorporated into the EEA Agreement through further Joint Committee Decisions amending Annex XX. The EEA Agreement framework means that the EFTA Surveillance Authority (ESA) monitors compliance in the EEA EFTA states, while the EFTA Court has jurisdiction over disputes — as demonstrated by the EFTA Court judgment in Case E-2/25 (November 2025) concerning ETS obligations.

Iceland negotiated a special arrangement to continue receiving free EU ETS aviation allowances beyond the standard phase-out schedule. In May 2023, at the Council of Europe summit in Reykjavik, Iceland and the European Commission reached a preliminary agreement for Icelandic aircraft operators to continue receiving free EU ETS emissions allowances up to and including the year 2026. This is one year beyond the general EU/EEA phase-out (where free allocation drops to zero from 2026). The Icelandic government argued that the standard rules harm the competitive position of Icelandic airlines and fail to account for Iceland's geographical isolation, which makes its citizens more dependent on air transport than other EEA residents. The EU sought to grant the Icelandic aviation sector an extra year of free allowances as a special solution compatible with the EU's broader climate goals. The agreement was subject to approval by EU Member States, the Icelandic government, and the Icelandic parliament (Althingi). Claims that the exemption extends to 'end of 2027' appear to conflate two different things: Iceland's free allocation exemption (through 2026) and the broader EU ETS aviation geographical scope limitation to intra-EEA flights (extended until start of 2027).

Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU) provides that any member state may decide to withdraw from the Union in accordance with its own constitutional requirements. The withdrawal process involves a two-year negotiation period, extendable by unanimous agreement of the European Council.
